justification: All pass criteria met: 11 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), already-shipped/delivered edge case (AC-5, AC-6, AC-7), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-8), authorization (AC-9), and race conditions (AC-11). At least 6 explicit ambiguities surfaced across low_confidence_areas and escalations — order status definitions, cancellation fees, who can cancel, payment model, cancellation window cutoff. confidence.level is 42, well below the 65 threshold. Edge cases section is extensively populated with 8 scenarios. Business rules are clearly labeled as assumptions with invariant_justification distinguishing hard constraints from policy decisions.

observations.text: 11 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, authorization, idempotency, and concurrency
confidence.level of 42 correctly reflects the extremely underspecified input
4 escalations explicitly surface key ambiguities: cancellation window policy, fee structure, payment model, and who can cancel
low_confidence_areas lists 6 unknowns including exact statuses, fee existence, multi-package support, and prepaid vs pay-on-delivery
Assumptions are explicitly labeled as assumptions in the confidence section, not presented as facts
BR-7 idempotency for already-cancelled orders is a strong edge case catch
Race condition edge case (courier pickup vs cancellation) demonstrates deep systems thinking

Some business rules (BR-3 tiered fees, BR-5 support-only for In Transit) are presented with moderate authority despite being pure assumptions — though they are flagged as non-invariant and the escalations do surface the ambiguity

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), already-shipped/delivered (AC-5, AC-6), already-cancelled (AC-10), and many more edge cases. At least 6 explicit ambiguities surfaced across confidence.low_confidence_areas and escalations (order lifecycle states, fee structure, who can cancel, partial cancellation, time window, regulatory context). confidence.level is 45, well under the 65 threshold. Business rules like cancellation fees (BR-3, BR-4, BR-5) are marked invariant:false with justification that they are policy decisions, and the assumptions section explicitly lists them. edge_cases section is richly populated with 9 scenarios including race conditions, idempotency, and system outage handling.

observations.text: 12 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, every order status, auth/authz, idempotency, and validation
5 formal escalations raised covering order lifecycle ambiguity, user identity, fee policy, partial cancellation, and regulatory context
confidence.level of 45 appropriately reflects the extremely underspecified PM input
Assumptions are explicitly enumerated in the confidence.assumptions array rather than silently baked in as facts
edge_cases section includes 9 well-reasoned scenarios including race conditions, double-click idempotency, and transactional outbox patterns
Business rules clearly distinguish invariants (true domain constraints) from policy (invariant:false) — no invented rules presented as immutable facts

Acceptance criteria AC-3 and AC-4 describe specific fee behavior (refund minus fee, fee breakdown UI) which is somewhat prescriptive given that fees are flagged as undefined — could have used conditional language like 'if fee policy applies'

Invented a 'cancellation reason required' rule (BR-7) and baked it into AC-11 without it being in the PM spec — though it is marked invariant:false and listed as an assumption, it could mislead downstream agents

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation in a logistics/courier context. A user (sender or potentially receiver) requests to cancel a previously placed delivery order. Cancellation has cascading effects on dispatch, driver assignment, package handling, billing, and refunds. The PM spec is extremely vague — it does not define WHO can cancel, WHEN cancellation is allowed, what constitutes an 'order,' or what happens to in-flight physical processes.","bounded_context":"Order Manag

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), already-shipped/in-transit rejection (AC-4, AC-5), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-6), plus authorization, race conditions, and more. At least 6 explicit ambiguities surfaced across low_confidence_areas and escalations (cancellable states, refund/fee policy, actual state names, who can cancel, time windows, partial cancellation scope). confidence.level is 45, well below the 65 threshold. edge_cases section is thoroughly populated with 8 scenarios. While Bird does invent detailed business rules (cancellation fees, specific state names, refund policies), every one is explicitly flagged as an assumption in the confidence.assumptions array and escalated for stakeholder confirmation — none are presented as facts.

observations.text: 10 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, authorization, race conditions, and idempotency
confidence.level of 45 correctly reflects the underspecified input — well below the 65 threshold
3 detailed escalations with options and recommendations for the key ambiguities (cancellable states, refund policy, actual state machine)
6 low_confidence_areas explicitly flag unknowns including exact states, fee existence, refund policy, and partial cancellation scope
Already-cancelled edge case handled as AC-6 with idempotency consideration
Authorization question addressed both as BR-1 and AC-7 — who can cancel is covered
All invented rules (fee structures, state names, point-of-no-return) are clearly marked as assumptions, not presented as facts

Time window restriction is not explicitly called out as an ambiguity — the closest is 'time elapsed' mentioned in a fee option, but there's no direct escalation asking whether a time-based cancellation window exists independent of state

The 'who can cancel' question defaults to owner-only (BR-1) without escalating whether operations staff should also have cancellation authority — this is flagged as an invariant rather than an ambiguity
